coredata.dc:title: Nanosized zingerone-triggered anti-angiogenesis contributes to tumor suppression in human hepatocellular carcinoma
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Kung, Mei-Lang
Huang, Shih-Tsung
Tsai, Kuo-Wang
Chu, Tian-Huei
Hsieh, Shuchen
coredata.dc:description: Suppression of tumor angiogenesis has become one of the most important means of antitumorigenesis, tumor development, and metastasis. Therefore, developing novel anti-angiogenic agents for tumor therapeutics is an important issue. Phytochemicals possess numerous effective antitumorigenesis properties and can inhibit cell growth and metastasis and are thus regarded as promising candidates for use as chemopreventive agents and chemotherapeutic adjuvants. In our previous study, we fabricated phytochemical-derived carbon dot-zingerone nanoparticles (zingerone NPs) and demonstrated their potent antitumorigenesis in vivo and in vitro against human hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC). In this study, we further validated the roles of zingerone NPs in antitumor angiogenesis. Here, two human endothelial cell lines, EA.hy293 and HUVECs, and two hepatocellular carcinoma cell lines, SK-Hep-1 and Huh7, were utilized to investigate the effects of zingerone NPs on antitumor angiogenesis. Our data demonstrated that zingerone NPs significantly inhibited the viability of human endothelial EA.hy296 cells and HUVECs. Moreover, zingerone NPs revealed superior anti-angiogenic effects on cell invasiveness, in vitro tube formation, and ex vivo rat aorta ring angiogenesis. By verifying zingerone NP-mediated molecular mechanisms of anti-angiogenesis, we found that zingerone NPs significantly decreased the protein expression of VEGF and VEGFR in endothelial cells. Moreover, zingerone NPs downregulated VEGF expression in hepatoma cell lines. The hepatoma Huh7 xenograft model also showed that zingerone NPs markedly attenuated tumor angiogenesis through inhibition of VEGF and CD31 expression. In addition, we also demonstrated that zingerone NPs significantly suppressed tumorigenesis by inhibiting VEGF-mediated PI3K/Akt/mTOR axis signaling and Akt/eNOS signaling in endothelial and hepatocellular carcinoma cells. These results suggest that the phytochemical-derived carbon dots-zingerone NPs possess superior characteristics in antitumor angiogenesis and antitumorigenesis. Altogether, our study provides recent advances in zingerone NPs as anti-angiogenic agents to enhance chemopreventive and anticancer eﬀects for future tumor therapeutic strategies.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Human hepatocellular carcinoma cell -Huh7 xenograft model and immunohistochemical analysis
Zingerone NP-mediated anti-angiogenesis in vitro and ex vivo is involved in hepatoma suppression
Zingerone NP-mediated downregulation of VEGF signaling is involved in the suppression of tumor angiogenesis in vivo and in vitro
Downregulation of PI3K/Akt/mTOR axis signaling is involved in zingerone NP-mediated anti-angiogenesis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Effects of zingerone NPs on the ability of hepatoma cells to form colonies on soft agar. To analyze the effects of zingerone NPs on the anchorage-independent colony formation of hepatoma cells, cells (SK-Hep-1 and Huh7) were grown in 0.35% agar-contained with various doses of zingerone and/or zingerone NPs for 7–10 days. Colonies were then subjected to staining with crystal violet and a diameter greater than 70 µm were counted and quantified. Representative images of colonies of (A) SK-Hep-1 and (B) Huh7 cells in soft agar. Scale bars: 50 µm. (B) Quantitative analysis of colony numbers was counted for each sample (n = 3) in (C) SK-Hep-1 and (D) Huh7 cells, respectively. Data were expressed as the mean ± SEM.* p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01.

Effect of nanosized zingerone on cell invasiveness of human endothelial-like permanent EA.hy926 cells and human hepatoma SK-Hep-1 and Huh7 cell lines. Cells were treated with various doses of zingerone and/or zingerone NPs and then subjected to Boyden chamber assay. By incubation for adequate time, cell invasiveness of (A) EA.hy926 cells, (B) SK-Hep-1 cells, and (C) Huh7 cells were imaged and recorded using inverted microscopy. Scale bar: 100 µm for EA.hy926 cells, and scale bar: 50 µm for SK-Hep-1 and Huh7 cells. The invasive cells of (D) EA.hy926 cells, (E) SK-Hep-1 cells, and (F) Huh7 cells were further counted and quantified. Bars represented as mean ± SEM for n = 5 membranes in three separate experiments. * P < 0.05; ** P < 0.01.

Effect of zingerone NPs on tube formation. The endothelial-like EA.hy926 cells and human endothelial HUVEC cells were treated with different concentrations of zingerone and/or zingerone NPs and added to 24-well plates precoated with Matrigel for adequate incubating time. Capillary-like networks of (A) EA.hy926 cells and (B) HUVEC cells were photographed and recorded using a light microscope (magnification, ×100). Scale bar: 100 µm. The numbers of the tubular ring structures of (C) EA.hy926 cells and (D) HUVEC cells were further counted and quantified. Data were expressed as the mean ± SEM. ** p < 0.01.

Effect of zingerone NPs on the vascular sprouting from rat aorta rings. Rat aorta arteries were treated with various doses of zingerone and/or zingerone NPs for 7–10 days. Representative images and (B) quantitative analysis of vascular networks in rat aortic ring explants. Data were presented as the mean ± SEM (n = 5) in three separate experiments. * P < 0.05; ** P < 0.01.

Effects of zingerone NPs on the expression profiles of VEGFA and VEGFR2 in EA.hy926 cells and human hepatoma SK-Hep-1 and Huh7 cell lines. Cells were treated with various doses of zingerone and/or zingerone NPs for 24 h. (A) The cell lysates were collected and detected the protein expression levels of VEGF and VEGFR using Western blot analysis in EA.926 cells. (B) The supernatants of EA.926 cells were further collected and subjected to detect the secretory VEGF protein using ELISA assay. Moreover, the indicated angiogenic VEGFA were also determined in (C) SK-Hep-1 and (D) Huh7 cells, respectively. Data were represented as means ± SD in three separate experiments. * , P < 0.05; ** , P < 0.01.

Effect of zingerone NPs therapy on anti-angiogenesis in NOD/SCID Huh7 xenograft mouse model. Immunohistochemistry staining for (A) CD31 and (B)VEGF in hepatic tumors after zingerone and/or zingerone NPs treatment for 21 days. (n = 15; ** p < 0.01).

Effects of zingerone NPs on the PI3K/Akt/mTOR pathway and Akt/eNOS signaling in endothelial-like EA.hy926 cells. Cells were treated with zingerone and/or zingerone NPs for 24 h. (A) Cell lysates were harvested and subjected to detect the Akt-mediated mTOR and eNOS signaling using a Western blotting assay. Protein expression profiles of (B) Akt, (C) mTOR, and (D) eNOS were further quantitated and analyzed using Image-Pro Plus and GraphPad Prism software, respectively. Data were represented and executed in at least three separate experiments. * , P < 0.05; ** , P < 0.01.

Effects of zingerone NPs on the PI3K/Akt/mTOR pathway in human hepatoma SK-Hep-1 and Huh7 cell lines. Cells were treated with zingerone and/or zingerone NPs for 24 h. Cell lysates were harvested and subjected to detect the Akt-mediated mTOR signaling in human hepatoma (A) SK-Hep-1 cells and (C) Huh7 cells using a Western blotting assay. Protein expression profiles of (B) SK-Hep-1 cells and (D) Huh7 cells were further quantitated and analyzed using Image-Pro Plus and GraphPad Prism software, respectively. Data were represented and executed in at least three separate experiments. * , P < 0.05; ** , P < 0.01.

Schematic representation of the molecular mechanism underlying the anti-tumor angiogenesis of phytochemical-derived zingerone NPs in human hepatocellular carcinoma. (A) VEGF signaling plays an important role during tumor angiogenesis. Tumor angiogenesis can be initiated by orchestrating various signaling pathways including PI3K/Akt/mTOR signaling and Akt/eNOS signaling between the endothelial cells and the tumor cells. (B) Zingerone NPs significantly decline the protein expression of VEGF and VEGFR and inhibit angiogenic tube formation and angiogenesis of endothelial cells. Moreover, the VEGF-mediated signaling pathways including the PI3K/Akt/mTOR signaling and Akt/eNOS signaling are also involved in the zingerone NPs-mediated antiangiogenesis in human hepatocellular carcinoma.

head.ce:title: Nanosized zingerone-triggered anti-angiogenesis contributes to tumor suppression in human hepatocellular carcinoma

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Suppression of tumor angiogenesis has become one of the most important means of antitumorigenesis, tumor development, and metastasis. Therefore, developing novel anti-angiogenic agents for tumor therapeutics is an important issue. Phytochemicals possess numerous effective antitumorigenesis properties and can inhibit cell growth and metastasis and are thus regarded as promising candidates for use as chemopreventive agents and chemotherapeutic adjuvants. In our previous study, we fabricated phytochemical-derived carbon dot-zingerone nanoparticles (zingerone NPs) and demonstrated their potent antitumorigenesis in vivo and in vitro against human hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC). In this study, we further validated the roles of zingerone NPs in antitumor angiogenesis. Here, two human endothelial cell lines, EA.hy293 and HUVECs, and two hepatocellular carcinoma cell lines, SK-Hep-1 and Huh7, were utilized to investigate the effects of zingerone NPs on antitumor angiogenesis. Our data demonstrated that zingerone NPs significantly inhibited the viability of human endothelial EA.hy296 cells and HUVECs. Moreover, zingerone NPs revealed superior anti-angiogenic effects on cell invasiveness, in vitro tube formation, and ex vivo rat aorta ring angiogenesis. By verifying zingerone NP-mediated molecular mechanisms of anti-angiogenesis, we found that zingerone NPs significantly decreased the protein expression of VEGF and VEGFR in endothelial cells. Moreover, zingerone NPs downregulated VEGF expression in hepatoma cell lines. The hepatoma Huh7 xenograft model also showed that zingerone NPs markedly attenuated tumor angiogenesis through inhibition of VEGF and CD31 expression. In addition, we also demonstrated that zingerone NPs significantly suppressed tumorigenesis by inhibiting VEGF-mediated PI3K/Akt/mTOR axis signaling and Akt/eNOS signaling in endothelial and hepatocellular carcinoma cells. These results suggest that the phytochemical-derived carbon dots-zingerone NPs possess superior characteristics in antitumor angiogenesis and antitumorigenesis. Altogether, our study provides recent advances in zingerone NPs as anti-angiogenic agents to enhance chemopreventive and anticancer eﬀects for future tumor therapeutic strategies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the molecular mechanism underlying the anti-tumor angiogenesis of phytochemical-derived zingerone NPs in human hepatocellular carcinoma.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Angiogenesis is indispensable during physiological and pathological processes, including embryo and organ development and wound healing, as well as tumor growth, progression, and metastasis.[1,2] Angiogenesis plays an important role in tumor development, providing a nutrient supply and guaranteeing oxygen sufficiency deep within tumors.[3] Therefore, novel drug development against tumor angiogenesis has become one of the main strategies for cancer therapeutics.[4] Angiogenesis is a very complex multistep process that can be initiated by tumor hypoxia and inflammation.[5] A series of biochemical signaling transductions orchestrated within microenvironmental cells such as endothelial cells, stromal cells, smooth muscle cells, etc., trigger new vessel generation or the production of additional vessels from preexisting vessels. Moreover, several angiogenic stimuli or proangiogenic factors participate in angiogenic initiation, such as vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF), angiopoietins (Ang), ﬁbroblast growth factor (FGF), platelet-derived growth factor (PDGF), matrix metalloproteinase (MMP) and hypoxia-inducible factor (HIF). Among these proangiogenic stimuli, VEGF is the key regulator of angiogenesis.[6–8].

To date, several signal transduction pathways have been shown to be involved in VEGF-mediated angiogenesis, including Raf-MEK-MAPK, P13K/AKT, and ERK1/2/FAK signaling, as well as hypoxia-inducible factor (HIF)-mediated hypoxia signaling.[4,5] Indeed, a previous study demonstrated that VEGF enhanced microvascular permeability through activation of PI3K/Akt signaling, endothelial nitric oxide synthase (eNOS), and mitogen-activated protein (MAP) kinases, which play a key role in normal neonatal vasculogenesis.[9] Among these VEGF-mediated angiogenic signaling pathways, phosphatidylinositol 3-kinase (PI3K)/Akt/mammalian target of rapamycin (mTOR) signaling has attracted much attention for targeted anti-angiogenesis strategies.[10] For example, previous studies have demonstrated that the natural polyphenolic compound apigenin can inhibit PI3K/Akt/mTOR axis signaling-mediated cancer cell growth, survival, and angiogenesis and thereby serve as a potent chemopreventive agent.[11] Moreover, molecular targeting of the PI3K/Akt/mTOR pathway using PI3K inhibitors, Akt inhibitors, and mTOR inhibitors has been suggested to be an effective means of therapy in hepatocellular carcinoma.[12].

Although numerous drugs have been developed and shown to have high efficacy against tumor angiogenesis, this antitumor strategy still does not completely eradicate cancers. Therefore, developing novel strategies based on anti-angiogenesis, such as in combination with chemotherapy, is one promising option for eﬃciently obstructing tumor progression and metastasis.

Phytochemicals are bioactive compounds obtained from dietary plants and/or plant sources such as herbs. Moreover, previous studies have demonstrated that some phytochemicals possess various potent activities, such as anticancer, anti-inflammatory, and antioxidative stress, promotion of apoptosis, and inhibition of invasion, migration, and metastasis, as well as anti-angiogenesis.[13] Therefore, all of these characteristics make phytochemicals important sources for cancer chemoprevention and adjuvant chemotherapy.[14,15] Zingerone is one of the phytochemicals isolated from the rhizome of ginger and identified as one of the non-volatile and pungent compounds (others including gingerols, shogaols, and parasols) in Zingiber ofﬁcinale. Zingerone can be obtained from drying ginger directly or through the thermal degradation of gingerols or shogaols. Zingerone is thus present in large amounts in ginger (approximately 9.25%) and has been demonstrated their multi-pharmacological activities, such as anti-cancer, anti-oxidation, and anti-inﬂammation in vivo and in vitro. Accordingly, phytochemical zingerone has identified its chemopreventive eﬀects in many cancer types including gastrointestinal cancer, colon cancer, and breast cancer [16,17].

Recently, nanomaterial-based antitumor strategies have received considerable attention.[18,19] By applying nanotechnology, nanotheranostic strategies are designed to treat various types of cancer. Carbon dots are carbon-based zero-dimensional nanomaterials that have attracted great attention due to their extraordinary physicochemical properties, including photostability, photoluminescence (PL), and different surface functional groups for easy further modification, all of which contribute to excellent water solubility, biocompatibility, lower toxicity, and environmental friendliness.[19] Recently, phytochemical-derived carbon dots (CDs), such as berberine-based carbon dots and curcumin carbon quantum dots, have been demonstrated to have superior efficacy in terms of antiviral activity and anticancer efficiency compared to their raw compounds.[20–22] Moreover, it was reported that the as-prepared ginseng-derived nanoparticles (GDNPs) possessed an effective antitumor effect, which was attributed to their capability to modulate M2 polarization and thus indicated their potential use as nanodrugs in cancer immunotherapy.[23] Indeed, in our previous study, we also demonstrated that as-fabricated phytochemical-derived zingerone nanoparticles (NPs) possessed superior tumor suppression and anti-invasion and anti-metastasis activities against hepatoma and oral squamous cell carcinoma (OSCC).[24,25].

Moreover, in our previous study, we found that the zingerone NPs elicited dramatical effectiveness in antiproliferation, antitumorigenicity, disturbing cell cycle progression, and induction of DNA damage as well as cell apoptosis in human hepatocellular carcinoma.[24] Here, we further explored the effects of zingerone NPs on the cell viability, motility and angiogenic capacity of endothelial cells. In addition, we also addressed their underlying molecular mechanisms, such as the VEGF-mediated PI3K/Akt/mTOR pathway and related downstream signaling, including eNOS signaling, in both endothelial cells and hepatoma cell lines.

Human hepatoma cell lines of SK-Hep-1 and Huh 7 cells were cultured in Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10% calf serum (CS), 100 IU/mL penicillin, 100 μg/mL streptomycin, and 2 mM L-glutamine. The endothelial cells of human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVECs) and EA.hy926 cells (a human endothelial cell variant) were purchased from BCRC (Food Industry Research and Development Institute, TAIWAN) and ATCC (Manassas, VA), respectively. HUVECs were cultured in M199 medium (Life Technologies, Gaithersburg, MD, USA) containing 15% fetal bovine serum (FBS) (HyClone, Thermo scientific, Logan, UT, USA), 20 U/mL porcine heparin (Sigma) and 100 μg/mL endothelial cell growth supplement (Calbiochem; La Jolla, CA, USA). HUVECs were used for all the experiments at passages 2–7. The EA.hy926 cells were cultured using Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM, Lonza) and were supplemented with 10% FBS (Gibco) and antibiotics (Primocin, Invivogen). These cells were incubated under humidified conditions in 95% air and 5% CO2 at 37 °C.

Bottom agar was made of 0.5% agar supplemented with 10% FBS in 12 well culture plate. Cells (4 ×103 cells) were mixed with 0.35% DMEM pheno-red free medium/agar supplemented with 10% FBS and various doses of zingerone and/or zingerone NP, and then placed onto the bottom agar. Cells were incubated to grow and form colonies for 7–10 days. Colonies were stained with 0.03% crystal violet for 30 min and the numbers of colonies were counted and photographed by microscope with digital images system (ZEISS Axio Vert.A1). A sphere size larger than 70 µm is regarded as a colony.

The invasion assay was achieved using a Boyden chamber assay as described previously.[25] Briefly, cells (2 ×105 cells/mL) were mixed with various doses of zingerone or nanonized zingerone in medium and then placed in the upper chamber (50 µl/well). The lower compartment was filled with 30 µl of medium containing 10% CS or FBS. A polycarbonate filter (8 µm pore size Nucleopore) was coated with Matrigel matrix (Corning, USA) and used to separate these two compartments. After adequate time courses incubation (16 h for SK-Hep-1 and Huh 7 cells, and 8 h for EA.hy926 cells), the membrane-contained invasive cells were subjected to methanol fixation and Giemsa staining (10% v/v, Merck). Finally, the fixed cells were photographed using microscope with digital images system (ZEISS Axio Vert.A1) and counted as mean ± SEM number of cells per filter under four different low-power fields.

24-well plates were first pre-coated with 100 µl of 0.5% agar contained with DMEM medium and 10% FBS. Next, a bottom matrigel was prepared with a ratio 1:1 of Matrigel (10 mg/mL, Becton Dickinson, Bedford, MA, USA) and cold serum-free culture media, and replaced onto the agar layer (250 µl/well). The prepared culture plates were next incubated at 37 °C for 1 h. Subsequently, HUVEC cells and EA.hy926 cells (5 × 104 cells/250 µl, culture media contained with zingerone and/or zingerone NP) were replaced onto the prepared Matrigel-coated plates and incubated at 37 °C for 4 h and 9 days for HUVEC cells and EA.hy926 cells, respectively. The endothelial tube formation was observed and photographed using an inverted microscope with a digital images system (ZEISS Axio Vert.A1). The number of endothelial tube lumens was counted in three replicate wells and only the completed ring structures created by three to five endothelial cells were considered as tubes.[26].

This ex vivo angiogenesis assay was performed as described previously.[26] Briefly, after the thoracic aortas were excised from 3-week-old Sprague-Dawley male rats (200–250 g; National Animal Center, Taipei, Taiwan), flushed with media, and removed the periadventitial fibroadipose tissue, Aortas were then cut into cross-sectional rings about 1–1.5 mm in length. Aorta rings were then placed onto a 48-well plate which pre-coated with 150 µl of cold Matrigel (Corning®, USA)-DMEM mixture (in ratio of 1:1), and then covered the rings with another 100 µl of Matrigel-DMEM mixture and incubated at 37 °C until the Matrigel polymerized. Finally, another 250 µl of cultured medium contained with zingerone and/or zingerone NPs was added onto aortic rings and maintained at 37 °C for 7–10 days. Microvascular sprouting was examined and imaged under a bright field phase contrast microscope (ZEISS Axio Vert.A1). The numbers of vascular sprouts were further recorded and analyzed in six different rings per treatment group.

The Western blot assay was executed as previously describe.[24] Brieﬂy, after the proteins were separated using 8–10% SDS-PAGE and then transferred onto polyvinylidene fluoride membranes (PVDF). The membranes were followed blocking procedure and then incubated with primary antibodies of VEGFR2 (Proteintech), and GADPH (GeneTex), eNOS, VEGFA (abcam), mTOR, and Akt (Santa Cruz Biotechnology), as well as p-mTOR (Ser2448), p-eNOS (Ser1177), and p-Akt (Ser473) (Cell Signaling, Beverly, MA) for 1 h at room temperature or overnight at 4 °C. After the HRP secondary antibody (Santa Cruz Biotechnology) administration, the signals were detected using Immoblon® Western Chemiluminescent HRP Substrate (Millipore) and exposed to X-ray film for autoradiography.

Effect of zingerone NPs on secretory VEGF was achieved using a human VEGF DuoSet ELISA assay kit (R&D Systems, Inc., USA). Briefly, EA.hy926 cells were seeded in 12-well culture plated (1 ×105 cells/well) for overnight and then treated with various doses of zingerone and/or zingerone NPs for 24 h. The conditioned medium was collected and centrifugated for subjected to ELISA assay according to the manufacturer’s instructions. The spectrometric absorbance at 450 nm was measured using a microplate reader (BioTek EON).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Human hepatocellular carcinoma cell -Huh7 xenograft model and immunohistochemical analysis
ce:section.ce:para: An animal model using human hepatocellular carcinoma Huh7 cell xenograft in NOD/SCID mice has been achieved and described in our previous study.[24] In this study, we used the remained specimens to analyze the effects of zingerone NPs on tumor angiogenesis. The paraffin-embedded Huh7 hepatoma tissue blocks were sectioned into 3-μm-thick slices and mounted on poly(lysine)-coated slides. After the slides were deparaffinized, they were blocked with 3% hydrogen peroxide for 10 min and subjected to antigen retrieval in 10 mM citrate buffer for 15 min in a microwave. The slides were incubated with a 1:50 dilution of CD31 and VEGF antibody (all from Santa Cruz Biotechnology, Santa Cruz, CA) at 4 °C overnight. After washed with PBS, they were incubated with horseradish peroxidase/Fab polymer conjugate (polymer detection system; Zymed Laboratories, South San Francisco, USA) for 30 min and detected using diaminobenzidine (1:20 dilution; Zymed Laboratories, South San Francisco, USA).

All animal care and experimental procedures were performed according to the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee (IACUC) of Kaohsiung Veterans General Hospital (IACUC Approval No. 2021–2025-A048) and ensured that all animals received humane care and that study protocols complied with the institution’s guidelines.

The statistical signiﬁcance of the data from the experiments or assays was determined using a two-tailed Student’s t test or one-way ANOVA. The statistical analysis was performed using GraphPad Prism 5.0 software (GraphPad Software, San Diego, CA). Data were presented as mean ± standard error of the mean (SEM) from indicated repeats of experiments. A p value less than 0.05 was considered statistically significant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Zingerone NP-mediated anti-angiogenesis in vitro and ex vivo is involved in hepatoma suppression
ce:section.ce:para: Angiogenesis is a crucial factor to provide nutrition support and oxygen during normal growth, development and healing. Tumor angiogenesis plays a pivotal role in tumor growth, progression, invasion, and metastasis.[4,27] Thus, anti-angiogenesis has also become a hot spot for developing tumor therapeutic strategies. Here, we first validated the effect of zingerone NPs on the in vitro tumorigenicity of human hepatoma SK-Hep-1 and Huh7 cell lines using an anchorage-independent colony assay. As shown in Fig. 1, cells were suspended in an agar solution containing various doses of zingerone and/or zingerone NPs and then spread onto soft agar substrates. These cells were allowed to grow at 37 ℃ for 7–10 days. Our results show that zingerone NPs significantly inhibited both colony numbers and colony sizes in a dose-dependent manner compared to zingerone for both SK-Hep-1 and Huh7 cells (Fig. 1 A-B). Our colony number data revealed that even the lowest dose of zingerone NPs (25 µM) could dramatically suppress colony formation by approximately 40% and 60% compared to zingerone treatment of SK-Hep-1 and Huh7 cells, respectively. The higher dose (over 50 µM) of zingerone NPs attenuated colony formation by approximately 50% and 90% compared to zingerone treatment (only 20–35% inhibition) of SK-Hep-1 and Huh7 cells, respectively (Fig. 1 C-D). These results suggest that zingerone NPs exhibit potent antitumorigenesis effects against human hepatoma SK-Hep-1 and Huh7 cell lines.

The phytochemical zingerone, which has cancer prevention properties and multiple pharmacological characteristics, was originally obtained from the nonvolatile pungent components in ginger.[16,17] In our previous study, we fabricated zingerone NPs through a thermal-condensation methodology and characterized the physicochemical properties of zingerone NPs using AFM, TEM, PL, and LCMS analysis. Consequently, we showed that the zingerone NPs formed nanotetramers and had an average size of 1.42 ± 0.67 nm.[24] As shown in Fig. S1A, zingerone NPs appeared as spherical particles with mono-dispersity. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used to further confirm the particle size and nanocomposite structure. The inset in (Fig. S1B) showed an HRTEM image of typical zingerone NPs and revealed a lattice spacing of around 0.21 nm, which is very close to the [100] in-plane lattice spacing of graphite carbon.[28] Next, by measuring the height of approximately 900 separate nanoparticles, the size distribution showed that the zingerone NPs have an average size of 1.31 ± 0.37 nm (Fig. S1C -D). These results indicate that this synthesis process of zingerone NPs is reproducible.

Cell migration and invasion are key factors for endothelial cells to initiate “sprouting” before generating new blood vessels and subsequently expanding the vascular tree.[3,29] By providing nutrients, growth factors, and abundant oxygen through vascular transportation, tumor cells can thus overcome the extracellular matrix barrier to migrate or travel into neighboring tissues and/or distant sites.[30] Here, we detected the effects of zingerone NPs on cell motility and invasiveness of endothelial cells and two hepatoma cell lines, SK-Hep-1 and Huh7. As shown in Fig. 2 A-C, compared with zingerone, zingerone NPs dramatically inhibited cell invasion of human endothelial-like permanent EA.hy926 cells and hepatoma SK-Hep-1 and Huh7 cells. For EA.hy926 cells, the lower dose of zingerone NPs (50 µM) treatment decreased cell invasion by 25%, whereas the higher dose (100 µM) of zingerone NPs treatment significantly inhibited cell invasion by approximately 75% compared to the zingerone group (Fig. 2 D). In two hepatoma cell lines, compared to zingerone-mediated finite reduction of cell invasion, zingerone NPs could significantly trigger the inhibition of cell invasion at the lowest dose of 25 µM. Moreover, following a dosage increase, zingerone NPs mediated a more severe inhibition of cell invasion in both Huh7 and SK-Hep-1 cells. This phenomenon was more obvious in Huh7 cells than in SK-Hep-1 cells (Fig. 2 E-F). These results indicate that zingerone NPs significantly attenuate cell motility and invasive ability of endothelial and hepatoma cells. Moreover, these data also imply that zingerone NPs may play a role in anti-angiogenesis.

To understand whether zingerone-mediated tumor suppression is attributed to endothelial cell survival inhibition and cellular dysfunction, we next analyzed the effect of zingerone NPs on endothelial cell viability and tube formation. By using the MTT assay, our data show that the lower dose of zingerone NPs (50 µM) could induce morphological changes in EA.hy926 cells and inhibit cell viability. Moreover, zingerone NPs inhibited cell survival in a dose-dependent manner, and the IC50 was 144.5 ± 5.2 µM (Fig. S2). To further analyze the angiogenesis of endothelial cells, EA.hy926 cells and human primary endothelial HUVECs were utilized to perform a tube formation assay. As shown in Fig. 3 A-B, these two types of endothelial cells induced web-like lumen structures in both the control and zingerone groups. However, these web-like lumen structures were destroyed by treatment with zingerone NPs in a dose-dependent manner. In EA.hy926 cells, even the lowest dose of zingerone NPs (25 µM) dramatically inhibited 75% of tube formation. Complete inhibition of tube formation could be achieved at the higher dose of zingerone NP (50 µM) compared to the zingerone group, which significantly induced tube formation (Fig. 3 C). In addition, a similar result was also found for HUVECs. The capillary-like tubes of HUVECs were well distributed in the culture plate during zingerone treatment, whereas administration of zingerone NPs impaired capillary-like tube formation and promoted cell death (Fig. 3 D). Indeed, by using an MTT assay, we found that zingerone NPs significantly suppressed HUVEC viability in a dose-dependent manner and obtained an IC50 of 115.1 ± 23.5 µM (Fig. S3). These results suggest that zingerone NP-mediated endothelial cell survival repression may contribute to the inhibition of angiogenic tube formation.

To further verify the effect of zingerone NPs on the angiogenic process, we evaluated zingerone NP-mediated ex vivo vascular sprouting efficiency using a rat aorta ring assay. Our data found that zingerone NPs exhibited remarkable anti-microvessel sprouting activity in a dose-dependent manner compared to the zingerone group ( Fig. 4 A). Compared to zingerone, which induced impressive vascular generation, even at the lowest dose (25 µM), zingerone NPs elicited potent anti-angiogenic effects. Complete anti-angiogenesis was found at the higher dose of zingerone NP (50 µM) (Fig. 4 B). Thus, these results indicate the potential of zingerone NPs in suppressing neovascularization ex vivo.

Phytochemicals and/or dietary components such as phenolic compounds, alkaloids, terpenoids, and carotenoids have demonstrated inhibitory eﬀects on cancer cells due to their impressive efficacy of anti-growth and anti-proliferation, invasion inhibition, and anti-angiogenesis. Phytochemicals are therefore considered to have possible uses as chemopreventive agents.[31,32] In addition, a previous study proposed that capillary endothelial cells could modulate tumor cell growth. Therefore, the inhibition of sprouting angiogenesis could facilitate tumor suppression.[33] Moreover, proangiogenic factor vascular endothelial growth factor-A (VEGFA), which can be generated in endothelial cells and overexpressed in most tumors, has been demonstrated to have pivotal roles in sprouting angiogenesis. Accordingly, interference with VEGF expression has been shown to suppress tumor growth in animal models.[34] In this study, we verified that phytochemical-derived zingerone NPs could significantly inhibit endothelial cell invasion, cell viability, and angiogenic tube formation and sprouting in endothelial cells at a low dose (i.e., 25 µM). In addition, zingerone NPs could significantly inhibit cell invasiveness in hepatocellular carcinoma cells. Therefore, phytochemical-derived zingerone NPs may have more potential to serve as antitumor angiogenic agents for cancer chemoprevention.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Zingerone NP-mediated downregulation of VEGF signaling is involved in the suppression of tumor angiogenesis in vivo and in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: Tumor angiogenesis comprises a complex multistep process involving interactions among tumor cells, endothelial cells, microenvironmental cells, growth factors, and extracellular matrix (ECM) components. Proteolytic degradation of ECM is an important event for cells initiating proliferation and/or migration. This process contributes to the tumor cell's subsequent invasion and metastasis as well as endothelial cell sprouting.[5,27] In this study, we showed that zingerone NPs significantly inhibited cell invasion of both endothelial cells and hepatoma cells (Fig. 2). Moreover, we also demonstrated that zingerone NPs markedly suppressed the survival of endothelial cells (Fig. S2 and Fig. S3) and inhibited angiogenic tube formation in vitro and ex vivo, respectively (Fig. 3 and Fig. 4).

Next, we elucidated the anti-angiogenesis mechanism of zingerone NPs. We first examined the effects of zingerone NPs on the proteolytic activity of matrix metalloproteinases (MMPs) in both endothelial cells and hepatoma cell lines. In endothelial EA.hy926 cells and HUVECs, by using a gelatin zymography assay, zingerone NPs significantly attenuated the activities of MMP-2 and MMP-9 in a dose-dependent manner (Fig. S4A -B). For hepatoma SK-Hep-1 cells, the data indicated that zingerone NPs obviously caused the downregulation of MMP-9 activity compared to MMP-2, whose activity was not affected by zingerone and/or zingerone NP treatment (Fig. S4C). Moreover, in contrast to SK-Hep cells, zingerone NP-mediated inhibition of MMP-2 activity in Huh7 cells was observed at the highest dose (200 µM). Interestingly, an upregulation of MMP-2 activity was found in cells treated with either zingerone or the lowest dose of zingerone NPs (Fig. S4D). These results suggest that zingerone NP-mediated downregulation of MMP-2 and MMP-9 activity in endothelial cells may play a role in disturbing tube formation. Moreover, zingerone NP-mediated cell survival inhibition in both endothelial cells and hepatoma cells may also contribute to the attenuation of MMPs and cell invasive suppression.

Matrix metalloproteinases (MMPs) are a family of zinc-binding and calcium-dependent neutral endopeptidases that can degrade most components of the ECM, have been implicated in invasive cell behavior and play an important role in the regulation of angiogenesis.[35,36] During angiogenesis, endothelial cells secrete MMPs to degrade the vascular basement membrane and remodel the ECM. In tumor cells, they can highly express MMPs to degrade the extracellular matrix and enhance their invasiveness and metastatic potential.[37,38] For example, tumor cells such as HCC cells and gastric carcinoma cells can synthesize and secrete MMP-2 and MMP-9 in a paracrine and/or autocrine manner to stimulate angiogenesis and increase vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) release, which contributes to epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT) and metastasis. Therefore, a previous study demonstrated that mice deficient in MMP-2, MMP-9, or MMP-14 exhibited reduced angiogenesis in vivo and in vitro.[39–41] Here, we demonstrated that zingerone NPs significantly decreased cell invasion and were associated with the downregulation of MMP-2 and MMP-9 activity in both endothelial cells and hepatoma cells. These results also imply that VEGF may play an important role during zingerone NP-mediated inhibition of cell invasion, MMP activity and angiogenesis.

Vascular endothelial growth factors (VEGFs) can be induced by angiogenic stimuli, such as cytokines, hormones, phorbol esters, oncogenes, and hypoxia, and can also be produced by tumor cells and endothelial cells. Additionally, VEGF is thought to be the most important proangiogenic factor and it can elicit cell growth, proliferation, migration, and angiogenesis through paracrine signaling. [42–44] To understand the roles of VEGF in zingerone NP-mediated suppression of angiogenesis, we analyzed the effects of zingerone NPs on the protein expression profiles of VEGF and VEGF receptor (VEGFR) in either endothelial cells or hepatoma SK-Hep-1 and Huh7 cell lines using western blot assays. As shown in Fig. 5 A, zingerone NPs attenuated the protein levels of VEGFA and VEGFR in a dose-dependent manner in EA.hy926 cells. Our data indicate that zingerone NPs significantly decreased protein expression (over 50% inhibition) in both VEGFA and VEGFR2 at 100 µM treatment. A similar result was also found in HUVECs. Our data revealed that zingerone NPs (100 µM) significantly inhibited the expression of VEGFA and VEGFR compared with the control and zingerone groups (Fig. S5). Moreover, by using an ELISA, we showed that zingerone NPs at a dose higher than 100 µM obviously decreased the secretion of VEGF protein by EA.hy926 cells (Fig. 5 B). These results suggest that zingerone NPs markedly disturb VEGF and VEGFR expression and are involved in endothelial anti-angiogenesis.

Next, we evaluated the effect of zingerone NPs on VEGF expression in hepatoma cells. Our results showed that zingerone NPs significantly suppressed VEGF expression in a dose-dependent manner in both hepatoma SK-Hep-1 and Huh7 cell lines (Fig. 5 C-D). Especially in Huh7 cells, we observed that the lowest dose of zingerone NPs (25 µM) could induce an obvious downregulation of VEGF expression (approximately 25%). The higher dose treatment (100 µM) elicited nearly 90% inhibition of VEGF expression (Fig. 5 D). These results suggest that zingerone NP-mediated VEGF downregulation is involved in the inhibition of cell survival, invasion, and tumorigenesis of hepatoma cell lines.

In our previous study, we demonstrated that zingerone NPs displayed potent antitumorigenicity using the Huh7 xenograft model in NOD/SCID mice.[24] To evaluate the effect of zingerone NPs on tumor angiogenesis in Huh7 xenograft hepatoma, two angiogenetic markers, CD31 and VEGF, were analyzed using immunohistochemistry. As shown in Fig. 6, either zingerone or zingerone NPs significantly attenuated the protein expression of CD31 and VEGF in Huh7 xenograft tumors. Moreover, compared to zingerone, zingerone NPs distinctly suppressed the expression levels of CD31 (Fig. 6 B) and VEGF (Fig. 6 B), which may be involved in anti-neoplasia and anti-angiogenesis activity.

The VEGF family comprises ﬁve members, including VEGFA, VEGFB, VEGFC, VEGFD, and placental growth factor (PGF), and they can bind to their receptors, such as the tyrosine kinases VEGFR1, VEGFR2, and VEGFR3, in an overlapping manner.[45] VEGFR1 and VEGFR2 are primarily expressed in endothelial cells and VEGFA, VEGFB, PGF, VEGFC, and VEGFD bind to these receptors.[46] Moreover, compared to VEGFR1, VEGFR2 possesses more potent proangiogenic activity and tyrosine kinase activity when binding to VEGFA. VEGFA mediates in vivo angiogenic responses primarily through the activation of VEGFR2.[45,47] Indeed, one of the anticancer strategies is targeting angiogenesis by blocking the interaction between VEGF and VEGFR using anti-VEGF or anti-VEGFR monoclonal antibodies, VEGF binding peptides or small molecular inhibitors of VEGFR tyrosine kinases.[48] In this study, we demonstrated that zingerone NPs significantly disrupted VEGFA and VEGFR2 expression by endothelial cells. Moreover, zingerone NPs dramatically inhibited VEGF expression by human hepatocellular carcinoma in vivo and in vitro. These results suggest that zingerone NP-mediated antitumor angiogenesis not only inhibits VEGF expression and secretion and VEGFR downregulation in endothelial cells but also depresses VEGF expression by hepatoma cells, which may contribute to tumor angiogenesis and tumorigenesis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Downregulation of PI3K/Akt/mTOR axis signaling is involved in zingerone NP-mediated anti-angiogenesis
ce:section.ce:para: PI3K/Akt and mTOR signaling play pivotal roles in the processes of carcinogenesis, including the regulation of cell growth, proliferation, invasion, and angiogenesis.[10] Accumulating studies have shown that abnormal activation of the PI3K/Akt/mTOR signaling pathway is involved in tumor development, including in hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) and renal cell carcinoma (RCC).[12,49] Moreover, Akt serves as a central factor to activate and/or guide numerous signal transduction pathways, including Akt-mediated eNOS signaling, which enables the regulation of endothelial cell proliferation, migration, and apoptosis suppression, as well as angiogenesis.[50] In this study, we demonstrated that zingerone NPs significantly attenuated VEGF-mediated cell growth and cell motility in endothelial cells and inhibited VEGF-induced tumorigenicity in vivo and in vitro.

Next, we evaluated the effects of zingerone NPs on angiogenesis-associated signaling mechanisms, including PI3K/Akt/mTOR signaling and Akt/eNOS signaling, in endothelial cells and hepatoma cell lines. We first analyzed the effects of zingerone NPs on the protein activities of Akt, mTOR, and eNOS in endothelial EA.hy926 cells. As shown in Fig. 7 A, compared with zingerone, zingerone NPs significantly downregulated the protein activities of Akt, mTOR, and eNOS in a dose-dependent manner. Our data indicated that the higher dose (100 µM) of zingerone NPs significantly decreased Akt and mTOR protein activity by approximately 50% (Fig. 7 B-C). Moreover, although zingerone could mediate the downregulation of eNOS activity by approximately 30%, even the lowest dose of zingerone NPs (25 µM) could decrease eNOS activity by approximately 40%, and doses higher than 50 µM could reduce eNOS activity by over 80% (Fig. 7 D). A consistent result was also found in HUVECs. Our data indicated that zingerone NPs markedly inhibited PI3K/Akt/mTOR signaling and attenuated Akt-mediated downstream signaling, including eNOS activity (Fig. S6). These results suggest that zingerone NPs suppress the PI3K/Akt/mTOR axis and Akt/eNOS signaling, playing pivotal roles in the angiogenic inhibition of endothelial cells.

Furthermore, we also validated the effects of zingerone NPs on PI3K/Akt/mTOR signaling in hepatoma cells. In SK-Hep-1 cells, zingerone NPs obviously inhibited the protein activities of Akt and mTOR ( Fig. 8 A). Obvious inhibition of these protein activities and/or protein expression was found at the higher dose of zingerone NP (100 µM) treatment. Additionally, unlike zingerone, which induced a significant upregulation of mTOR activity, zingerone NPs could downregulate mTOR activity in a dose-dependent manner (Fig. 8 B). In Huh7 cells, zingerone NPs distinctly disturbed the activities of Akt and mTOR (Fig. 8 C). As in SK-Hep-1 cells, zingerone NPs could attenuate the activities of Akt and mTOR at the higher dose of zingerone NPs (100 µM). In addition, zingerone NPs mediated the downregulation of mTOR activity in a dose-dependent manner, and a significant decrease in mTOR activity could be observed at the lower dose of 50 µM (Fig. 8 D). These results indicate that zingerone NPs significantly downregulate PI3K/Akt/mTOR signaling, which may therefore contribute to the inhibition of cell growth, proliferation, invasion, and angiogenesis of hepatocellular carcinoma cells.

In this study, we demonstrated that zingerone NPs significantly inhibited VEGF-mediated Akt/eNOS signaling in endothelial cells. Our data reveal that eNOS activity is highly susceptible to zingerone NPs compared to zingerone and imply that Akt/eNOS signaling may play a key role in zingerone NP-elicited anti-angiogenesis. Indeed, Akt-mediated eNOS phosphorylation and the subsequent induction of NO generation are key factors in the regulation of vascular tone, vascular remodeling, and angiogenesis.[51] In addition, VEGF-mediated Akt/eNOS signaling activation and upregulation of MAP kinase-dependent pathways can significantly enhance endothelial cell permeability in human umbilical vein endothelial cells.[9] On the other hand, since PI3K/Akt/mTOR signaling cascades exert key roles in cell proliferation and viability, migration, invasion, and angiogenesis, blocking mTOR signaling transduction using mTOR inhibitors as an anticancer therapeutic strategy has attracted great attention. mTOR is ubiquitously expressed in various cell types, including tumor cells, immune cells, and endothelial cells. mTOR inhibitors can disrupt VEGF generation by blocking mTOR-mediated VEGF signaling during the hypoxic tumor process, inhibiting IKKβ-mediated mTOR/VEGF signaling during tumor inﬂammation-mediated angiogenesis, and eliciting tumor-associated macrophage polarization to an anti-angiogenic phenotype. Moreover, interfering with mTOR signaling can effectively suppress cell survival, proliferation, and migration of endothelial cells.[10].

Biologically active phytochemicals, such as organic acids, phenolics, and terpenoids, can be extracted from different parts of the plant and they possess multipharmacological properties, including anticancer, antioxidant, antiviral, antibacterial, anti-inflammation, and anti-angiogenesis. Zingerone is a multipharmacological compound belonging to a member of the methoxyphenol family and its related derivatives and is a nonvolatile pungent compound derived from the rhizome of ginger (Zingiber oﬃcinalis).[16,17] In this study, we fabricated phytochemical-derived zingerone NPs and validated their effects on angiogenesis using endothelial cell-like EA.hy926 cells and primary endothelial HUVECs. Our results demonstrated that zingerone NPs markedly reduced cell viability and cell motility and impaired angiogenic tube formation in vitro and ex vivo. Additional molecular assays also verified that zingerone NPs significantly inhibited the protein levels of VEGFA and VEGFR2. Consequently, VEGF-mediated downstream signaling pathways, including the PI3K/Akt/mTOR signaling axis and Akt/eNOS signaling, all collapsed during zingerone NP treatment ( Fig. 9). Altogether, our study supports the use of zingerone NPs as an anti-angiogenic agent for enhancing chemopreventive and anticancer eﬀects in future tumor therapeutic strategies.

In this study, we demonstrate that phytochemical-derived carbon dot-zingerone NPs exert a superior anti-angiogenic effect and contribute to suppressing tumor angiogenesis and tumorigenesis. Our data show that zingerone NPs significantly inhibit cell viability, cell motility, and angiogenesis in vitro and ex vivo in endothelial cells. Moreover, zingerone NP-mediated attenuation of invasiveness and tumorigenesis was validated in hepatocellular carcinoma cells. Based on the cell functional validation, we further verified that zingerone NP-mediated downregulation of VEGF-elicited PI3K/Akt/mTOR axis signaling might block angiogenesis, metastasis and tumorigenesis. These novel findings provide insight into how phytochemical-derived zingerone NPs prevent angiogenesis and tumorigenesis and could be useful for enhancing chemoprevention and chemotherapeutic strategies.
